data.title: Snap: Underwriting Tail Risk For A 21% Yield Ahead Of An Inevitable Restructuring
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Snap recently had a substantial setback with its stock price due to massive stock-based compensation (SBC) and Specs cash burn, which has sent the stock down well below its historical average. The market's disappointment has not only driven down its stock price, but also pushed implied volatility (IV) of options to high levels. However, investors ignore the fact that Snap is a special asset. It can be a highly profitable company if it changes. Furthermore, if the stock price falls excessively to current levels, change is inevitable. The management has acknowledged this and taken action, taking a step to establish Specs as a distinct subsidiary within Snap Inc. in Feb 2026. This situation has attracted me to try to create an options strategy with a very thick safety margin and an annualized return of up to 21%. In the following analysis, I will delve into the underlying logic behind this trade.

Business Overview

Snap includes two business units: Snapchat and Specs.

SNAPCHAT

Snapchat is a very good and popular product, as highlighted by Irenic Capital in their “Snap Back to Reality” presentation. Advantages of Snapchat include:

  • 1 of only 4 scaled U.S. social networks

  • ~1bn MAUs and 474mm DAUs –Huge, irreplaceable scale

  • $6bn in revenue growing 10%+ YoY

  • $1bn in subscription revenues growing 60%+ YoY

  • Unique ability to captivate user attention and media trends

  • Proprietary data assets and advertising stack –one of the few “walled gardens”

The current price of Snap is $4.63, implying that TEV is only $8.4 Bn, and TEV‑to‑Sales ratio is 1.4x. Compared to its competitors, it is extremely undervalued (Reddit is 6.6x, Meta is 5.2x, Pinterest is 2.0x), which is the consensus in the market. These undeniable facts form the basis for all the following discussion.

Specs

Specs is another story. On the one hand, it is the technological dream of Evan Spiegel. In the letter of “Crucible Moment”, Evan Spiegel describes it as "human-centered computing" , claiming that:

"Specs represent a shift away from the app paradigm to an AI-first experience... into a once-in-a-generation transformation towards human-centered computing."

However, on the other hand, financially, it is a high-risk project with no near-term profitability. According to Irenic's estimates, a total of $3.5 billion was invested in R&D over 11 years, and by 2025, an estimated $500 million in cash plus $200 million in SBC had been invested. Furthermore, CEO Evan Spiegel himself has publicly admitted that a true commercial breakthrough for AR glasses won't happen until the end of the decade (2030).

Reasons for the stock price setback

Snapchat and Specs are two completely different businesses. The company used operational cash from SNAPCHAT and a large amount of SBC to support Specs. This kept the company in a negative profit state for a long time, and its stock price fell by 77% compared to its IPO. Shareholder returns gave way to the founders' technological dreams.

At the same time, the founders held 20% of the shares but had 99% of the voting rights. This voting structure meant that external shareholders could not effectively impact the founders when the company neglected shareholder returns. This also lowered the company's valuation.

Catalyst for change

In SNAP's business model, SBC is a key factor. We can see from the table as following:

Annual stock-based compensation expense is approximately $1 billion. If paid in cash alternatively, EBITDA in 2025 would be -$320 million, and operating cash flow would become - $340 million, which will close all debt financing channels for the company.

According to estimates, Specs' cash burn in 2025 will be approximately $700 million, meaning that most of the cash generated by SBC will be used for Specs. If the SBC plan is unsustainable, Specs will also lose a stable source of funding.

However, the sustainability of the SBC plan depends on one key condition: a reasonably high stock price, allowing earnings growth to significantly outpace share dilution.

If we look at the SBC dilution rate in different stock price's condition, it is easy to demonstrate why the status quo is mathematically unsustainable.

Snap’s SBC Dilution & Required Growth vs Stock Price

As shown in the table above, if the stock price remains at the current price of $4.60, the dilution rate reaches 12.86%, which is four times the dilution rate of 3% in 2025. This means that theoretically, EBITDA growth needs to be significantly higher than 12.86% for the stock price to remain unchanged. In an extreme case, if the stock price falls to $3, the dilution rate reaches 20%, meaning that theoretically, EBITDA growth needs to be significantly higher than 20%.

If the EBITDA growth cannot be met with a high dilution rate , the stock price will continue to decline. On the one hand, the SBC plan will become unsustainable, and Specs will lose a stable source of funding. On the other hand, the value of employee stock options will continuously shrink, severely damaging morale and causing talent loss. In that case, Specs will also fail.

Our opinion is that in this situation, Evan Spiegel will inevitably make structural changes to balance company profitability, shareholder returns, and technological dream; otherwise, it will be a lose-lose situation.

Evan Spiegel is a Silicon Valley genius, but he could get carried away with his technological dreams. However, he also has extensive business experience, and Snap has an experienced board of directors. He completely understands it, emphasizing this point in as follows:

Achieving net income profitability would also help offset the dilution risk of stock-based compensation and establish a stronger foundation under our share price. These are the facts of our crucible moment.

From letter to employees “crucible moment” in 2025

We continued to manage our share count carefully, with share repurchases completed throughout 2025 helping limit share count growth to 3% in Q4.

From Snap Investor letter Q4 2025

Snap has spent $500 million repurchasing shares to support stock price and offset the dilution. Also declare a new $500 million repurchasing plan in 2026. All of this proves that the company recognizes the impact of stock price on SNAP's business model.

The Path Forward

Snapchat is a top-tier social media product with 1 billion MAUs. If management focuses on software monetization and cost control, the company should be able to easily achieve profitability. Furthermore, Snapchat has strong growth potential in subscription revenues and data licensing. If the cash-burning Specs business were spun out, as an independently operating company, Snapchat would become a highly profitable company with high free cash flow, thereby unlocking the company's value.

Meanwhile, based on current predictions, Specs is unlikely to achieve cash flow breakeven before 2030. A rational approach would be to spin it out and operate it as a startup. Operating independently, Specs could rationally assess its business prospects and optimize efficiency based on hardware product development cycles; at the same time, it can obtain a suitable valuation. Attracting external capital and establishing its own SBC system.

The company has recognized this and taken action, taking an important step to establish Specs Inc. as a distinct subsidiary within Snap Inc. in Feb 2026.

Specs faces a challenging environment in the current capital markets, particularly regarding securing independent funding and establishing its own SBC system. However, this actually approves our opinion: if external capital declines to fund Specs, it will put its future commercial viability to a real test. Without external funding, together with the unsustainable nature of Snap's internal SBC system due to the depressed stock price, management will be forced to significantly reduce or even halt Specs investment. Therefore, regardless of whether Specs ultimately thrives as an independent company or is forced to scale down due to funding shortages, the final result is the same: cash burn stops. Which will unlock the value of Snap's core business. This effectively limited the further downside potential of Snap's stock price.

My Strategy: Execution Plan to Capture Risk Premium Using Asset Dislocation

I sold Cash-Secured Puts with a $3.00 strike price. The expiration date is Jan 2027. The option premium is $0.36. If unexercised, the total annualized return is >21%. If the exercise occurs, the actual entry price is $2.64 per share. In that scenario, I am acquiring Snap at a $5.6 billion TEV.

In my strategy, I always act like a stock price risk insurer. If the exercise occurs, my logic is the same as that of a non-performing asset disposal company.

Execution Details and Return Analysis

I sold Cash-Secured Puts with a $3.00 strike price. The expiration date is Jan 2027. Under the premise of 100% cash collateral (zero leverage), the annualized return of this option is 17%. Total Return: If adding the current 4% margin short-term T-bill interest income, the total annualized yield is >21%. This is a highly asymmetric risk underwriting opportunity based on extreme low TEV plus high IV in the current market environment.

Internal Logic: Abnormal Premium of Implied Volatility (IV)

The current implied volatility (IV) of Snap reaches 80%. It means that the market thinks the possibility of a "cash burn out plus more share dilution" is very high in Snap. The 1.4 TEV‑to‑Sales ratio has priced the stock with high risk. At the same time, high IV priced the options wrongly again. According to my analysis, the $4 Snap price is far below the bottom line required for both Snap’s SBC operation modes and Specs’s survival. The probability of this happening is extremely small. The high IV is the market paying too high a premium for "extreme tail risk of burning money endlessly without structural change." It is obviously an overpricing of risk.

Risk Bottom Line Test Breakeven Point

The breakeven point for this strategy is $2.64. At that time, the company's TEV was only $5.6 billion. TEV‑to‑Sales ratio is 0.93

Assignment Preparation

In extreme cases, even if the option is exercised, I am acquiring Snap with $5.6 billion in TEV. I believe I still can get good returns in the end. At this time, I am in non-performing asset disposal company mode.

I have built real $3.00 strike Put positions. This position is part of my "Special Situations Arbitrage" portfolio. This portfolio includes 30 uncorrelated targets with similar logic. I hold this position without regard to short-term stock price fluctuations. My target is to generate 20%+ annual underwriting profit by identifying asset pricing, risk pricing error and Potential catalysts. In the extreme case of Snap, I think an annual return of 21%+ can far cover the actual loss for potential risks. This is one of my typical ways to get high annualized returns with actual low risk in special situations arbitrage.

data.title: Target: My Target Price Justifies The Recent Rally, Pause For Now
data.description:
If you remember, I advised you not to do anything after Target Corporation (TGT) rose and delivered 12% returns in my previous coverage. I admit that I spoke both as an analyst and as an investor in this company as I suggested to existing investors not to sell their shares and to newcomers not to touch TGT yet. My view of its valuation paid off as the price reached my target price using the Dividend Discount Model. But of course, I realized I was a bit too early to downgrade it. And now that the market is becoming more volatile amid riskier external factors, I believe that it's essential to revisit this stock. Valuation and technicals now appear more synchronized as upside potential now appears limited.

Target: Some Strengths Amid Challenges

Macroeconomic volatility has remained intense in recent months. Although inflation has slowed down, it remains above the 2.0% Fed target. In addition, stubborn inflation means prices are still increasing. So, this also continues to squeeze purchasing power as well as pricing flexibility of businesses. Retailers like Target Corporation are not safe from these headwinds. If you look back to my previous coverages, it has experienced softer demand in many of its products/niches. Even in its most recent performance, the negative impact of softer discretionary spending was evident.

In Q4 2025, its sales amounted to $30.4B, down by 1.5% YoY from $30.9B. This YoY decrease was the same as in my previous coverage, which means that TGT continued to grapple with sales recovery. The percentage decrease may only be a single digit, but $500M was still crucial for the company. The consolation is that this was slightly better than in H1 2025, as sales dropped by 1.9%. This can tell us that TGT was able to cope better with softer market conditions. If you look at its sales per segment, beauty and food and beverage increased, which partially offset the weakness in other product categories. Its advertising segment also remained strong.

We may also infer that TGT reduced its volume in weaker categories so as not to save costs. And I think it was effective, as COGS decreased by 2.0% and completely offset the sales decrease. As a result, the gross profit margin increased to 26.6% versus 26.1% YoY. The problem was that its operating expenses still increased, so the operating margin decreased to 4.5% versus 4.7% YoY. Nonetheless, TGT still derived enough revenue to cover all costs and expenses and remain profitable.

Potential Risks

As a retailer, TGT stays highly sensitive to inflation. And the problem may escalate due to the Middle East tension. Why? Oil prices are already skyrocketing, so fuel prices may most likely follow. This will make the transportation of its products more expensive. Additionally, fuel prices may affect multiple industries, which may exacerbate inflation on top of tariff woes. With that, more consumers may reduce their discretionary spending. Additionally, it may have to raise wages so employees can keep up with the rising prices. With that, inflation may squeeze its sales growth and margins further.

Competition with cheaper alternatives like Amazon (AMZN) and Walmart (WMT) must be considered since they can take away a portion of its demand. E-commerce versus driving to TGT stores may offer more convenience and savings. So, this is no longer just a matter of pricing battles but also value propositions.

Potential Risk Mitigants

Before I proceed, I would like to highlight that these are purely risk mitigants, not growth drivers. I believe that these things can help TGT survive these headwinds or cushion their impact. However, these may not be enough to drive its growth and rebound.

One aspect to consider is its business/pricing model. TGT built its own identity through its product lines and pricing. It offers diverse categories that cater mostly to middle-income households. So, its pricing sits in the middle of premium retailers and cheaper alternatives like WMT and dollar shops. It also has its own branding of products to maintain customer trust and loyalty. And I think this continues to work in some ways if you look at its resilient performance in food and beverage, advertising, and beauty. However, it will be challenging for TGT to lower its pricing to attract demand without sacrificing its margins.

Meanwhile, it may derive some benefits from its physical stores. Despite its efforts to adapt to the digital revolution, TGT is still mostly a brick-and-mortar business. This can be capital-intensive and cost-sensitive. Even so, its high concentration may still have some bull cases. For instance, this can serve as a strong asset backing, which can provide some downside protection. In fact, the net value of the PP&E is nearly half of its EV of $70.15B. This can also lead to some monetization flexibility. For instance, TGT can do sale-leasebacks. In short, TGT is not only a retailer but also a real estate business.

Lastly, it still maintains robust liquidity, which assures us of its sustainability and longevity. Its cash and cash equivalents remain high at $5.5B. This should not be surprising since its operating cash inflow is still enough to cover capex. Also, a substantial portion of its operating expenses are noncash items like depreciation, amortization, and share-based compensation. TGT complements it with prudent debt management through timely issuances and repayments. More importantly, it maintains a decent maturity profile. As you can see, only 12% of the total amount will mature within the year. Also, it has more than enough cash to cover it. This is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.4x, which means that it only needs 1.4 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. Hence, it has enough capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is $4.56 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $1.14. The dividend growth is 7.42%, which I derived using the five-year CAGR. Meanwhile, I lowered its risk premium to 6.5% because I realized that 7.0% is already too high and too conservative relative to the 5.0% market standard. Lastly, its Beta is only 1.02, which means that the stock has become less volatile than in my previous coverage. But this is quite higher than 1.00, which means that the range of price swings is still a bit wider than the S&P 500. With all this information, I derived a TP, or target price, of $126.32. This justifies the continued price rally to its current price. But at this level, the gap between the TP and the current price is already narrow. So, upside potential may become limited.

Its price ratios also suggest that TGT is still a bit cheap today. Its P/E shows that the stock trades at only 15.03x its EPS versus the five-year average of 18.09x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $147.10. However, it is important to note that 2022 was an outlier. If we only focus on 2021, 2023, 2024, and 2025, TGT is trading within the range of the previous years. It's not exactly that cheap. Additionally, its EPS growth YoY was negative. If we get the EPS growth using the 2022-2025 CAGR, it will be 8%. However, the PEG is still high at 1.88 (15.03/8), which means that TGT is still fully priced at this level. Hence, I still advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Technicals

TGT's technicals are still bullish as it moves with higher highs and lows. In my previous coverage, I also failed to note the shifting trend of the 50-day SMA after crossing above the 200-day SMA. This was a crucial aspect since it could have signaled uptrend continuation. That also began when the price crossed above both the 50-day and 200-day SMAs. Now, the stock price is still trending above the two SMAs, which suggests that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. However, you can also see increasing selling pressures in the past month. It is also worth noting that the market is testing its resistance as the stock trades within $120-124. If the stock price does not break above it, the breakout attempts may weaken. So, it's also important that the stock price does not go below $112-114 for the uptrend to hold. With that, I advise caution at this level.

Its MACD also advises caution as it moves downwards. This signals early bearish divergence, which means that fewer buyers are entering as the stock becomes more expensive. Meanwhile, the histogram is still within positive territory, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But note that it is decreasing again close to zero in line with the weakening MACD line. This can tell us that selling volume is starting to catch up again with buying volume. Lastly, RSI in the past two months showed that the stock has become overbought. The trend is also showing a specific range, which also verifies that the market is becoming more cautious at this price.

Conclusion

Target Corporation stays resilient with its prudent cost management, well-diversified business model, and strong liquidity. However, these are not enough to drive further upside, as they are merely risk mitigants amid macroeconomic headwinds. Valuation also appears almost fully priced relative to its dividends and earnings growth, which may limit its upside potential. Technicals are still quite bullish, but recent overbuying may strengthen selling pressures. I'm reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Alignment Healthcare's Diagnosis Improves After Industry Developments, But Not Enough For An Upgrade
data.description:
April 7th was a really interesting day for shareholders of Alignment Healthcare (ALHC). Shares of the company closed up 16% after some interesting industry news came out. No company-specific developments occurred. What we do know, at least those who follow the company closely, is that management has been growing the business at a rapid pace. These industry developments should, in the long run, bode well for the company. But in the short run, there is the possibility of some margin compression. Having said that, it's clear that the market has turned bullish on the firm, at least in the near term.

At the time this article is published, it will have been exactly one year to the day since I last wrote about Alignment Healthcare. In that article, published in April of 2025, I ended up calling the business a ‘hold’ candidate. I found the business model to be fascinating. However, the stock did not offer the fundamental health to justify a bullish outlook at that time. Since then, shares have risen 14.4%, which unfortunately has underperformed the 33.3% increase that the market saw. Although this recent move up, the sad truth is that the stock is still not priced at levels to justify a bullish assessment. So for now, the best thing that I can do is to reaffirm it as a ‘hold’ candidate.

A health check for Alignment Healthcare

Given the amount of time that has passed since I last wrote about Alignment Healthcare, I think a bit of detail regarding what the company is and what it does is warranted. At its core, the firm is a consumer-centric platform that focuses on improving healthcare experiences for seniors. It does this by offering up its own Medicare Advantage plans that are customized specifically with seniors in mind. The firm utilizes certain predictive algorithms that are fed to it using medical and demographic data in order to identify the risks of a significant medical event on a customer-by-customer basis. And then, through the use of its Care Anywhere clinical teams, it provides the care in question for those with chronic conditions with the goal of reducing hospital admissions and readmissions in the long run.

When I was in college, I was fortunate enough to take a graduate-level health care economics course. I found the industry to be fascinating. And one thing that stuck in my mind was how the preventative care approach taken by countries with universal healthcare systems ultimately improves health outcomes in the long run while reducing costs, even if it means that short-term costs might increase. Although Alignment Healthcare does not operate in a universal healthcare system, what it is trying to accomplish is very reminiscent of that.

And operationally, the company also has an intriguing business model because it is set up to receive premiums from the federal government that are based on its contract with the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, or CMS for short. This is an amount that is based on a per-patient basis. The firm then promises to cover all of the health care costs for its customers, no matter how high those costs might end up being. There are certain risk adjustment factors baked into this, so not every customer results in the exact same dollar amount coming to the company. But that only makes sense given the risks involved. And, even with some disparity, the end result is more reliable cash outflows for the federal government. The downside to this is that Alignment Healthcare assumes the risk associated with those patients. But this is something that management has been working out rather nicely in recent years.

The newest data that investors have access to covers through the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business was $3.95 billion. That's well above the $2.70 billion generated a year earlier. The fact of the matter is that the number of health plan memberships that the company has had increased during this time from 189,100 to 236,300. A larger membership base naturally translates to greater earned premiums. However, the company has also benefited from higher revenue per member per month. This can be attributed to an increase in the CMS benchmark rates, as well as Medicare Part D revenue from changes that came courtesy of the Inflation Reduction Act.

One thing that is incredibly important to note here is that profit margins for this type of industry will theoretically remain rather small. And that's because management has to pay out most of what it receives in the form of revenue to health care providers. That's why, back in 2023, the business generated a loss of $148 million. In 2024, it was $128 million. But last year, the loss amounted to just $0.7 million. Other profitability metrics improved also. From 2024 to 2025, operating cash flow rose from $34.8 million to $139.9 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we get an improvement from negative $26.1 million to positive $94.4 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business went from $1.3 million to $109.9 million.

Obviously, higher revenue helped here. But another contributor ended up being an improvement in its Medical Benefits Ratio, or MBR for short. For the uninitiated, the MBR is calculated by dividing total medical expenses, excluding depreciation, medical equity-based compensation, and restructuring costs, by the revenue that the company generates for any window of time. When you strip out those costs, you are left with what is essentially the gross profit from the Medicare Advantage plans that the company offers. Well, year over year, the MBR declined from 88.8% to 87.5%. That might not seem all that significant. But when you are already talking about a firm that is low margin by nature, it has a substantial positive impact on the bottom line.

Management also seems optimistic when it comes to 2026. They expect, for instance, for the number of members that they have to grow from 236,300 to between 292,000 and 298,000. This actually represents an increase over previous guidance, with that guidance calling for the low end of the range to be 290,000. Overall, this should result in revenue for the business of between $5.135 billion and $5.19 billion. And from that, the company anticipates that EBITDA will grow from $109.9 million last year to between $133 million and $163 million this year. In all likelihood, this will translate to adjusted operating cash flow of around $127.1 million.

Long term, there does seem to be some growth potential here. And the company recently received some help in this regard. On April 7th, news broke that the average Medicare Advantage payments administered by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services will be boosted by 2.48% in 2027. This dwarfs the 0.09% increase that the federal government had proposed earlier this year that negatively affected the share prices of the companies that operate in this space. This alone is a huge benefit for the company, not only for the long haul but for the near term as well. This means that management will be in a better financial position in order to cover unmet costs. But while that is a positive across the board, there is one other item that could have a short-term negative impact, though it could translate to greater top-line and bottom-line growth in the long run.

Also on April 7th, America’s Health Insurance Plans, which operates as the largest trade group that represents US health insurers, said that leading health plans had removed 11% of prior authorization requirements since previously pledging in 2025 to streamline the process in question. This is particularly noteworthy because pre-authorizations by insurers require that healthcare providers obtain coverage approvals from their patients’ health plans before proceeding with certain non-emergency medical procedures. But in June of last year, the trade group agreed to simplify and streamline the prior authorization process. A survey conducted by the Blue Cross Blue Shield Association also found that there had been a greater than 15% reduction in prior authorizations when it came to Medicare Advantage plans.

Prior authorizations have long been an important tool of insurers because they give the company the opportunity to deny care. This ultimately improves margins, while a reduction in pre-authorizations should compress them. But for a company such as Alignment Healthcare, this could actually be a positive development even if it might mean somewhat lower margins in the near term. By emphasizing a data-driven approach that allows him to tackle the higher-cost members that they are likely to receive anyways, the company’s cost management advantage over traditional insurers already puts it in a favorable competitive position, which should theoretically permit it to gain even more market share in the long run.

This is important because this is already a large and growing space. Management calculated that, in 2025, there were an estimated 35.49 million people enrolled through Medicare Advantage. That's up materially from the 17.50 million that were enrolled 10 years earlier. By 2034, we should see a further increase because of an aging population and a continued transition toward Medicare Advantage plans to 46.55 million people. When you add on top of this trend the fact that the company continues to invest in various growth and cost-cutting efforts, such as by expanding its team of AI engineers and data scientists, investing in clinical personnel for the next phase of its clinical innovations in the palliative care management and concierge case management markets, investing in the deployment of new clinical programs, and even expanding into new markets, there is plenty to be optimistic about.

If management does achieve the targets that they set out for the 2026 fiscal year, we can see in the chart below how shares are priced at the moment. These are rather lofty multiples. And this is where we run into the problem that I have with the business. Although I am incredibly bullish about it from an operational standpoint, the valuation of the business is extreme. In that chart, you can see how the business stacks up against six firms that it considers to be comparable to it. Now, to be clear, there are significant differences between some of these businesses and it. But this is the list that management gave, and I don't think it's necessarily a bad one. Regardless, from an evaluation standpoint, Alignment Healthcare is drastically more expensive than these other businesses. So much so that I would typically take a bearish position if it were not for the growth prospects that exist here.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that the market appropriately rewarded Alignment Healthcare and its investors for the news items that came out. In the long run, I am optimistic about the business from an operational standpoint. This is especially true now that cash flows are positive. But until we see some improvement from the valuation standpoint, I believe that the best thing I can do is to rate the business a ‘hold.’

data.title: An Unsurprising De-Escalation Favors The Bulls, For Now
data.description:
On Monday, I stuck my neck out with a prediction that the correction lows in the major market indexes were behind us. Stocks opened sharply lower yesterday after President Trump reiterated his threats to wipe out the Iranian civilization at 8 p.m. last night if his demands were not met to fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Yet investors bid up stocks throughout the day until the S&P 500 managed to turn green minutes before the closing bell. Unsurprisingly, the President announced after the close that he had agreed to a two-week ceasefire, which was orchestrated by the prime minister of Pakistan.

Evidently, this ceasefire includes Iran, the United States, and Israel, assuming Iran fully reopens the strait, which it appears they have agreed to do. As a result, oil prices plunged, with WTI falling some $18 to $94.50, while Brent is down more than $15 to $94. Gold and silver are surging, and S&P 500 futures are up nearly 3%. The dollar is giving back its gains, and Treasuries are rallying with yields falling, as investors begin to see an interest rate cut from the Federal Reserve back on the table for this year. It has paid to stay on the bullish side of the aisle during this conflict, but volatility is bound to continue in a world that is far less certain, which argues for maintaining cash in portfolios to be opportunistic.

In another sign of economic resilience, orders for durable goods declined 1.4% in February, but when we exclude the volatile transportation category, sales rose 0.8% and 6% over the past year. Most importantly, shipments of non-defense capital goods excluding aircraft, which is the measure used to calculate GDP, rose 0.9% in February. We are currently at an annualized run rate in the first quarter that is 5.3% above the fourth quarter and should lift the rate of economic growth. Businesses continue to spend at a healthy rate.

Corporate insiders, who clearly know more about their businesses than anyone else, also remain on the bullish side of the aisle, given that net insider buying rose to 25% of companies in March. That was during the heat of the war in Iran, and it is an increase from February.

Now we have to consider the bad news, which is the temporary surge in headline inflation that will result from the five-week war, assuming it comes to a resolution over the next two weeks. The Cleveland Fed estimates that the Consumer Price Index (CPI) will surge 0.84% for the month of March, while the personal consumption expenditure (PCE) price index will increase 0.61%. These are huge increases, but there will not be a meaningful impact on the core rates, which exclude food and energy.

There have also been global supply chain disruptions that will put upward price pressure on a variety of other goods and services in the months to come. If the war concludes in two weeks, then the Federal Reserve will likely look past these temporary increases, allowing the central bank to resume rate cuts later this year when Fed officials are certain that disinflation is back on track. It will take months to restart the production of oil and gas in the Middle East, which means prices for both are unlikely to fall to pre-war levels any time soon. This will slow global growth, and I will be looking for any signs from corporate management teams that forward earnings guidance may be adversely impacted.

I still do not think that this geopolitical crisis will upend the U.S. economic expansion or bull market. Therefore, I remain in wealth accumulation mode for now, albeit with higher cash levels than before the crisis to take advantage of inevitable volatility in the spring and summer months. While we may have significantly damaged Iran’s military capabilities, they have now effectively nationalized the Strait of Hormuz and intend to charge fees for transit. The old regime has been replaced with a new one that is just as extreme. The worst-case scenario looks to be off the table, but I have serious doubts about whether the world is better off than it was before. That is likely to limit meaningful upside in the market beyond today’s huge reflex rally.

data.title: Forgent Power Solutions: Powering On Post Its Public Offering
data.description:
When Forgent Power Solutions (FPS) went public early in February, I wondered if shares had the power. Currently, shares have been trading largely flattish in a $26-$37 price range, with shares exchanging hands around the $30 mark.

Compared to the time of the offering two months ago, my regard for the business has improved a great deal, in between accelerating growth, margin gains, strong guidance, a strong order intake, and remarks about long-term growth and capacity.

This is highly encouraging in my view, amidst a relatively stable share price while market conditions at large are difficult. This makes me quite upbeat here. My regard for the business has increased a great deal, and dips from here certainly look compelling enough to buy into dips.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Recent News

In the final days of March, Forgent sold a total of some 34.5 million shares at $29.50 per share. Nearly 24 million shares were sold by selling shareholders, with the company offering 10.8 million shares, including the overallotment option here.

The offering came relatively soon after the offering, and Forgent reported second-quarter results in the middle of the month. That was the first public report of Forgent as a publicly traded business.

These numbers look quite solid at face value, with reported sales up 69% to $296 million, driven by data center and grid customers. GAAP operating profits of $20 million were up minimally on the year before, suggesting real deleverage in percentage terms. Quarterly revenues rose a bit from a $283 million number for the first quarter, yet GAAP operating margins have come in quite a bit from a $31 million number in the first quarter.

With interest expenses largely coming in around levels of operating profits, the company posted largely break-even results. Those are the GAAP results, with adjusted earnings for the quarter seen around $35 million. Much of the reconciliation seems fair, but on the other hand, earnings are quite aggressive in the number of adjustments being made.

Of course, the results were applicable for the period that ended on the 31st of December, so ahead of the public offering taking place.

Note that the mixed results, that of sequential growth in sales and pressure on margins, contrast with momentum seen elsewhere in the business. Quarterly bookings of $762 million come in at more than 2.5 times reported sales! The backlog rose by 100% on an annual basis to $1.5 billion, indicating that topline growth might accelerate and likely margin gains can be delivered upon going forward.

For the year, the company sees full-year sales between $1.275 billion and $1.325 billion, with adjusted EBITDA seen between $300 and $310 million. Based on the performance year to date, the implied guidance for the remaining two quarters of the year suggests quarterly revenues of $360 million and adjusted EBITDA of $90 million. That looks rather strong, with the second quarter adjusted EBITDA reported at $60 million.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

The company ended the second quarter with a net debt load of $477 million, as the company has been investing quite a bit in its new facilities. The company sees this carrying a capacity to support $5 billion in annual revenues, with maintenance capital spending seen dropping to just about 1 percent of sales. Following the IPO at $27 per share, the company has seen solid demand for the manufacturing of various electrical distribution equipment, these being "engineered to order" applications. Typical applications are seen in data center power distribution, utility substations, and various energy-intensive manufacturing applications. In fact, data centers make up over 40% of sales in 2025, with grid customers making up nearly another quarter of sales. Needless to say, such solutions are in great demand, as is evident in the revenue (growth) numbers, but moreover, the backlog numbers, which show even more momentum here. For the valuation part, it should be seen that private equity firm Neos has been active with M&A in the "construction" of Forgent, having built up the business through a series of acquisitions here.

At the time of the offering, I believed that there were some 304 million shares outstanding. Shares sold by selling shareholders are already included in this number, as Forgent itself has sold nearly 11 million shares recently, for a 315 million pro forma share count. In between $432 million in gross proceeds from the IPO (excluding the overallotment option), the existing net debt load, and recent equity issuance, I see pro forma net cash holdings around $300 million.

Trading at $30, the market value stands at about $9.5 billion, for a $9.2 billion enterprise valuation.

On Fire

Based on the outlook for the second half of the year, revenues trend around $1.44 billion, valuing the business at just over 6 times sales. Adjusted EBITDA trends around $360 million, for a 25 times multiple on that front. Both of these are quite solid numbers, much stronger than I anticipated in February, certainly as it was not clear which role the M&A played in the pace of growth. In the absence of recent M&A here, growth seems to be accelerating, which is looking good.

Moreover, with capital spending seen as minimal going forward, real earnings power is seen here, in between a net unleveraged balance sheet. That suggests that earnings could come in close to around a dollar per share. All this is much stronger than I believed in February, all of which is quite comforting with earnings power rapidly providing support to the valuation here.

Moreover, earnings are near-term depressed, with revenue numbers trending softer compared to booking numbers, which trend closer to $3 billion based on the number reported this quarter. Furthermore, the company has a large runway for growth, with its current capacity seen as facilitating $5 billion in sales.

Despite the current rapid growth trajectory, the company is only producing at around 30% of capacity. This is very encouraging; certainly, as the company needs to absorb the extra capacity of the new factory and is hiring ahead of a ramp-up in sales, leading to suboptimal near-term margins.

Given all this, much more potential remains in the work. I have become much more appealed to Forgent here, even as the company needs to spend a remaining $73 million in terms of capital spending to complete the factory here.

Strong momentum is very welcome, and my regard for the business has improved a great deal compared to the time of the offering, given the accelerating growth, convincing outlook, strong backlog, and positive remarks about capacity and future growth.

data.title: Invesco American Franchise Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The market experienced a period of digestion

Growth equities underperformed value during the quarter after outperforming since the Trump administration's April 2nd tariff announcements, as markets adjusted to the reality that AI funding would require companies to raise capital on an ongoing basis.

2 Massive capital spending on AI is creating opportunities and challenges

We believe the AI investment cycle is still in its early innings and may involve potential for shorter-term volatility.

3 We believe active management will be important in this environment

Strategic stock selection will be particularly crucial in navigating this market environment and the AI innovation wave, in our opinion.

Investment objective

The fund seeks long-term capital appreciation.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M) 17,493.44

Portfolio managers

Ido Cohen, Ronald Zibelli

Manager perspective and outlook

  • US equities saw renewed volatility in the fourth quarter amid economic crosscurrents and mixed investor sentiment toward artificial intelligence (AI) valuations and AI-related spending.
  • The longest government shutdown in US history further dampened confidence, as key economic data were delayed. Once released, the initial third-quarter GDP estimate showed robust growth of 4.3%. However, labor market conditions softened.
  • Consumer spending remained resilient but showed signs of strain among lower income households. Inflation moderated but stayed above the US Federal Reserve's (Fed) 2% target.
  • Against this backdrop, the Fed cut the federal funds rate twice during the quarter, lowering the target range to 3.50% - 3.75%.
  • We have a constructive outlook for US equities, supported by a resilient economy, strong corporate profits, reduced regulatory burdens, tax cuts and tax credits, as well as Fed interest rate cuts.
  • If 2025 was about building the AI foundation, we believe 2026 is poised to be about scaling and monetizing AI across the global economy.
  • Despite potential for short-term volatility, we believe AI will be one of the largest investment cycles of the last 100 years and we estimate this cycle is still in its early innings, particularly when viewed through the lens of forward growth.
  • As always, we remain agile and data-driven, ready to adjust fund positioning as conditions evolve.

Portfolio positioning

At quarter end, the fund's largest overweights were in industrials, energy and communication services. Within the industrials and energy sectors, AI-related energy demand continues to drive electrical infrastructure and power generation supply chain spending. The fund's industrials exposure also includes companies poised to benefit from higher global defense spending. We favor communication services for its AI-driven monetization potential. The largest underweight was health care, due to loss of Affordable Care Act subsidies and reduced health care insurance expected in 2026. Information technology (IT) is also an underweight due to substantial underweights in Apple and Microsoft, relative to their very large index weights. Financials, which should benefit from stimulus from tax cuts and bank deregulation, was close to equal weight.

New Positions

Argenx (ARGX) We sought to increase biotech exposure with what we consider the best large-cap option. We believe Argenx is catalyst rich over the next six months, with five phase III FDA readouts coming, and should also benefit from lower interest rates.

Caterpillar (CAT) Power is 25% of company equipment sales, which is ideal for the backup and bridge power applications data center hyperscalers need for the AI build-out. Mining is also an important market for the company and the recent bid for metals will be a tailwind for investment. Lower interest rates are another tailwind.

Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) This leading multinational electronic brokerage firm benefits from the tailwind of market volatility and increased use of digital tokens to represent real-world assets.

Notable Sales

KKR (KKR) and Blackstone (BX) Stocks of alternative asset managers like KKR and Blackstone have been struggling technically and have tended to decline when there are credit concerns, which is increasingly likely. Also, neither KKR nor Blackstone will benefit from growing use of digital tokens, which we see as an important theme for 2026.

Oracle (ORCL) Oracle may not see clarity on its partner investments for some time. Also, it has a much higher legacy debt load than other companies in the database solutions space. We rotated this capital to chip maker Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) following its strong analyst day.

Boston Scientific (BSX) Boston Scientific makes minimally invasive medical devices such as pacemakers, catheters and stents. We still like the company, but we thought the bulk of its growth was behind it for the time being and sold the position in favor of stocks where we had higher conviction.

data.title: U.S.-China Trade Falls Despite Being Propped Up By Soybean Exports
data.description:
The United States' top export to China is soybeans. That's why the news the U.S. and China had struck a trade deal at the end of October 2025 was so exciting, because it came with a commitment from China to buy 12 million metric tons of U.S.-grown soybeans.

China's soybean buyers completed their purchases before the end of 2025, the bulk of which were exported in December 2025 and January 2026. But instead of giving a large boost to trade between the two nations, that increase in exports only delivered a small increase over the November 2025 low, with the boost offset by the falling level of trade of other goods.

In February 2026, the boost from the negotiated soybean sales to China ended, and the combined value of goods exchanged between the U.S. and China fell to $26.9 billion. That level falls below the 2020 coronavirus pandemic low for this data series.

The following chart shows that negative data trend for the monthly value of trade between the U.S. and China.

We had anticipated a larger boost to that overall trade from China's soybean purchases from the U.S., but that impact has proven to be disappointing.

It's clear the level of trade won't return to its pre-2025 tariff war levels anytime soon. The trailing twelve-month average of that trade is $18.8 billion below our counterfactual projection of what the level of trade between the U.S. and China would look like in the absence of the trade war between them in February 2026.

That brings the decline in the trailing twelve-month average of the direct trade between the U.S. and China to a cumulative loss of $108.5 billion in the value of goods exchanged between the U.S. and China.

Meanwhile, reports indicate China is turning to alternatives to reduce its need to import large quantities of soybeans, which, if successful, will negatively impact soybean farmers in both the U.S. and Brazil, the largest exporters of soybeans to China:

At the edge of one of the many pig farms spread across the vast, unbroken floodplains of Taizhou, a two-hour drive northwest of ​Shanghai, a pair of square, four-metre pools of acrid-smelling ochre liquid hold the key to cutting costly soybean use in half.

The pools hold a swill of cheaper, locally sourced ingredients, which ‌can include brans, pumpkin vines and wine lees. But it is fermented - like yogurt - so the proteins are already broken down and easy to digest, lessening the need for the higher-quality proteins in soy, 80% of which China imports....

The government sharply accelerated a drive ​to expand protein sources for livestock in March of last year, just as trade tensions ramped up early into President Donald Trump's second term. Soybeans quickly became a ⁠key bargaining chip.

Reuters interviews with dozens of livestock and feed producers, state researchers and industry experts revealed Beijing is moving faster than previously thought to deploy new technologies and promote fermented feed.

It's the agricultural equivalent of Beijing's campaign to build ​domestic capabilities in microchips and artificial intelligence, catalysed by Washington's stringent controls on advanced technology exports to China.

A permanent loss of soybean exports would further hammer the level of trade between the U.S. and China.

References

U.S. Census Bureau. U.S. International Trade in Goods and Services (FT900). U.S. Trade in Goods with China, Not Seasonally Adjusted, Nominal Figures, Total Census Basis. [Online database]. Accessed 19 February 2026.

data.title: ADT: Business Needs To Show Earnings Growth
data.description:
Investment Overview

I wrote about ADT Inc. (ADT) previously with a hold rating, as the underlying fundamentals were not holding up well. For instance, customer retention was soft, dealer volumes were weak, and also the State Farm catalyst was not playing out as I expected. The slowdown I was worried about has now shown up in the reported numbers, and the FY2026 guide suggests the near-term earnings bridge is still messy. That said, I am not ready to turn bearish because ADT is still generating strong cash, holding healthy margins, and has enough financial flexibility to keep investing while returning capital to shareholders.

The Slowdown Happened as Expected

I had a cautious view on ADT in my previous update because the underlying operating metrics were not painting a picture of growth. While total revenue still grew 7% y/y to $1.287 billion, and adj. EBITDA grew 7%, churn had already moved up to 12.8% (20 bps sequential increase), and the pace of State Farm ramp was not great. Turns out, I was right. For those that missed the Q4 earnings numbers, here are some data for reference. Revenue growth slowed sharply, from 7% in Q2 2025 to just 1.3% y/y in Q4 2025. Security Installation and Other did continue to grow, but the pace of growth slowed from 44% in Q2 to just 10% in Q4.

The FY2026 guide did not help to ease concerns regarding this slowdown either. As a recap, management guided flat revenue and flat adj. EPS growth for FY26, but they also guided for churn to worsen to 13.1%, and the bridge to this is not great. Specifically, the multifamily divestiture reduced 4Q recurring monthly revenue [RMR] by $2.6 million, or ~$30 million on an annualized basis. On top of that, there are $50 million of added growth investments and a $45 million tariff headwind. As you can see, this is not one clean issue with one clean fix. It seems to me part of the drag is structural, part of it comes from management choosing to invest harder, and part of it is really external. There are just too many moving parts here that make the near-term earnings bridge not clean.

This is also why I think the medium-term targets are not getting much credit right now. After this rather horrendous quarter, and with management guiding flat revenue and flat adj. EPS growth for FY2026, investors are not going to anchor on the longer-term guide first. My expectation is they are going to ask whether the current business is good enough to support those long-term targets (5% revenue growth, ~10% adj. EPS growth, and 10% free cash flow growth). This is not entirely impossible, but as of where we are today, those targets are just not something the market will underwrite today.

Counterbalance View to Support a Neutral Rating

As to why I am not hasty to downgrade ADT to a sell is because this is still not a business with collapsing economics. This is still a business that prints $670 million adj. EBITDA in Q4 and has adj. EBITDA margin of 52.5%. It is also printing a good chunk of adj. free cash flow ($139 million). Of course, this is not to say fundamentals will improve, but this gives ADT the financial flexibility (or, aka, balance sheet strength) to invest heavily to steer topline growth back to positive. I would also say there is still a decent amount of stability in the business model, with ~85% of revenue coming from recurring monitoring subscriptions. Unless churn really accelerates to, say, 20 or 30%, ADT should continue to print a sizeable amount of cash. Also, remember that ADT has a choice to just return more capital to shareholders (which helps support the share price). They have, in fact, expanded the share repurchase program to $1.5 billion over the next 3 years after repurchasing ~$600 million in FY25 (no repurchases in 4Q25). ADT certainly has no problem servicing this remaining $900 million, which, if it pulls forward, could imply a yield of ~17% in 2026.

The other reason is Origin AI. ADT acquired Origin AI for $170 million in cash and expects to bring the technology into its offerings in 2027. I think the logic is fairly straightforward. If the technology works as intended, it should make the service more useful (better motion analytics), help ADT hold on to customers better (better retention), potentially open up some higher-tier upsell, and take some cost out of the system by cutting down on false alarms. ADT also signed a five-year agreement with Verisure worth $30 million to continue licensing the technology. I would not model the financial impact of this with high confidence for now since the contribution is still unclear and the timing is not near-term. Still, it gives ADT at least one credible product-led path to improve the model later, especially on retention, which is one of the issues today.

Valuation

I believe the reason why ADT is likely to continue trading at a discount to its peers (those offering security services like Brink's Co. (BCO), CoreCivic (CXW), and Alarm.com Holdings (ALRM)) is really because of the recent growth weakness and FY2026. My base view is that investors hate uncertainty, and this is a situation where one cannot aggressively "buy in" to the long-term view since FY2026 could be another year of growth deceleration. Just comparing the multiples relatively, ADT trades at 4.8x forward EBITDA, while BCO, CXW, and ALRM trade at ~6.3x, ~6.7x, and ~8x, and these peers all have a higher EBITDA growth outlook (ranging from mid-single-digits to as high as 20%) vs. ADT at basically flat. My view is that multiples will stay range-bound for a while until ADT shows signs of growth acceleration.

Conclusion

I maintain a hold rating on ADT. In my view, the most recent numbers confirmed that the underlying slowdown was real, and the FY2026 guide did little to rebuild confidence in the near-term growth outlook. The reason I do not go more negative is that ADT still has solid cash generation, a stable recurring revenue base, and some longer-term product upside from Origin AI. My bottom line is that the stock likely stays range-bound until ADT can show better subscriber trends, improved retention, and a cleaner path back to growth.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity Freedom® Funds (the Funds) are designed so that the target date referenced in the Fund name is the approximate year when investors expect to retire.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom® Retirement Fund (FFFAX), each of the Funds seeks high total return until reaching its respective target retirement date; thereafter, each Fund's objective will be to seek high current income and, as a secondary objective, capital appreciation.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom® Retirement Fund, each Fund's asset allocation strategy becomes increasingly diversified as it approaches its target date – and beyond. Ultimately, the Funds are expected to merge with Fidelity Freedom Retirement Fund.
  • The Funds employ a disciplined and time-tested investment process focused on helping investors achieve successful retirement outcomes by leveraging the depth and strength of Fidelity's investment research and resources.

The U.S. Federal Reserve cut interest rates twice in Q4, given signs of softer employment conditions. Inflation remained above target. Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury bond yields finished Q4 effectively unchanged at around 4.2%. Credit spreads remained low relative to history.

See the Performance Summary table in this review for performance reporting on all vintages. Funds shown in this chart are representative samples of where a target-date investor might be at various stages of their life.

Performance Attribution Summary

  • Active asset allocation positioning and security selection added value to the fund's performance versus the Composite index in Q4. The fund's overweight in non-U.S. equities meaningfully contributed, as investments in both developed-markets equities and emerging-markets equities outperformed U.S. equities during the quarter.
  • The impact of active security selection varied by asset class. The strong performance of U.S. large-cap managers resulted in a relative contribution from U.S. equities, whereas non-U.S. managers, in aggregate, underperformed the Composite index.

Relative Contributors (representative across vintages):

Large-cap U.S. equity funds stood out to the upside this quarter, including the following notable relative contributors:

  • Fidelity® Series Growth Company Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Steve Wymer, who invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue-chip growth to aggressive growth. Steve focuses on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches, and those he considers capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Security selection within the information technology sector contributed to the fund's relative result this quarter, especially out-of-benchmark positions in Sandisk and Ciena, from the technology hardware & equipment industry. A non-benchmark position in private company Space Exploration Technologies, a maker of spacecraft and provider of satellite communications and launch capabilities, also notably contributed.
  • Fidelity® Series Large Cap Stock Fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P 500® index. The fund, managed by Matt Fruhan, emphasizes companies for which he believes long-term normalized earnings and free cash flow are underappreciated by investors. Security selection contributed to the fund's relative result, notably within the industrials and financials sectors. Within financials, overweight positions in Wells Fargo and Bank of America added relative value, while larger-than-benchmark stakes in GE Vernova and United Parcel Service stood out in the industrials sector.

Relative Detractor (representative across vintages):

The largest fund-level detractor:

  • Fidelity® Series Overseas Fund underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index. The fund is managed by Vincent Montemaggiore, who is valuation sensitive and focuses on higher-quality companies with durable growth. Security selection detracted this quarter, particularly within the industrials sector. Within the sector, an overweight in Thales, a French company that provides advanced technologies, services and products to the defense, aerospace, space and cyber markets, hurt the fund's relative result most. An out-of-benchmark position in Baycurrent, one of Japan's largest consulting firms, also notably detracted, as did an overweight stake in Japanese food and biotechnology company Ajinomoto.

Active Asset Allocation:

  • The fund outperformed the Composite index for the quarter, driven by active asset allocation positioning.

Outlook and Positioning

  • Target-date funds are long-term strategies designed to help keep participants on track toward their retirement goals through different market environments. Strategic asset allocation decisions are focused on providing age-appropriate asset allocation throughout participants' changing time horizons.
  • Our research highlights several macro-oriented themes that we believe underscore the need for portfolio diversification. Government debt, global fragmentation, aging demographics and new technology are likely to impact long-term growth and inflation. We believe diversification in fixed income and inflation-resistant assets remains attractive to hedge these risks.
  • In 2025, we announced an update to the glide path and strategic asset allocation of the strategies, based on our long-term views of participant behavior and our capital markets research. We increased exposure to equities for early-career investors and to equities and inflation-sensitive assets for investors near and in retirement. Implementation of the updated allocations is underway, and we expect to complete the transition within the next six to 12 months.
  • Active asset allocation positioning relative to the strategic benchmark reflects our intermediate-term views on assets that are mispriced relative to our estimate of fair value. Active risk in the funds is at the low end of the historical range, based on a view that elevated asset valuations, particularly for U.S. stocks, provide little cushion against near-term economic and geopolitical risk. We believe valuations outside of the AI epicenter are reasonable, and many sectors have improving growth expectations.
  • As of the end of 2025, the funds were overweight equities, albeit by a smaller magnitude since the start of the quarter. We maintain a preference for non-U.S. assets relative to U.S. assets, based on attractive valuations and the potential for non-U.S. assets to benefit from continued U.S. dollar weakness.
  • We added to fixed-income investments in the fourth quarter, and we view intermediate-term U.S. Treasuries as the most attractive part of the Treasury yield curve. The Funds remain underweight investment-grade bonds, as spreads are low relative to history.
  • Inflation remains sticky and upside risk may be underappreciated by investors. Supply-side indicators, including housing and labor markets, pressure the intermediate-term horizon, in our view. As a result, in Q4 we added to the funds' overweight in commodities.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: Moody's: Big Buybacks Are Creating A Floor Price
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Have you ever met someone who was extremely nice and pleasant to be around but was ultimately extremely forgettable? My wife and I had an acquaintance in college whom we enjoyed spending time with, but incidentally, we routinely failed to think of them when we were making plans. We didn't have anything against them, and while we were not exceptionally close friends with them, we would spend time with them occasionally. They were someone who just seemed to fall through the cracks within our social circle.

When it comes to investing, investors often struggle with this same problem. You see, we have companies we hear about regularly, and we just assume that because everyone is aware of them, they're going to be extremely overvalued or perhaps extremely unattractive as an investment at all. We would assume that because they're so well known, they will be extremely crowded companies, and the value is just not going to be there to be attractive. However, I have found that there are some companies that simply slip through the cracks of investors' minds because of those reasons, and the value remains attractive even though they're exceptionally well-known.

Likely, when it comes to a company's credit rating, you ask yourself what the credit rating is by Moody's or S&P Global. Yet do you invest in those companies that have such a massive, well-known reach, or do you look right past them?

A Legendary Business With a Tailwind Trifecta

Moody's Corporation (MCO) is a multifaceted business with multiple well-known segments. Readers are probably most familiar with its Moody's Investors Service or MIS segment, which forms a global duopoly alongside S&P Global (SPGI). Any time corporate debt is refinanced or new debt is raised, a fee is charged by these rating agencies. That comprised approximately half (53.4%) of the company's $7.72 billion in total 2025 revenue.

Then, there's the Moody's Analytics or MA segment. This is a subscription-based (SaaS) business that provides financial intelligence and analytical tools. That made up the remaining 46.6% of total revenue in 2025, with 95%+ of its revenue being recurring.

Moody's closed out 2025 with notable strength. The company's total revenue rose by 13% year-over-year to $1.89 billion in Q4 2025. This topped the analyst consensus for the quarter by $19 million.

That was made possible by a 16.9% uptick in MIS revenue to $946 million during the fourth quarter. This was powered by a surge in issuances from hyperscalers who tapped markets to fund AI capex and private credit transaction revenue momentum (+40% in Q4).

The MA segment reported $943 million in revenue in the fourth quarter, which was up 9.3% over the year-ago period. That was driven by continued AI adoption (Moody's GenAI solutions grew at twice the rate of the broader customer base, which demonstrates its value).

Moody's adjusted diluted EPS surged 38.9% higher year-over-year to $3.64 for the fourth quarter. This exceeded the analyst consensus during the quarter by $0.20. That was fueled by operating leverage in MIS (the adjusted operating margin expanded by 720 basis points to 58.5% in Q4). The scalability of MA was another factor, with the incremental cost of adding a new subscriber being minimal (the adjusted operating margin improved by 190 basis points to 35.7% in Q4).

Looking ahead, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted diluted EPS to compound by 12.3% annually through 2028, off a 2025 base of $14.94. Moody's is also a financially healthy company, sporting an A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

Moody's 0.9% dividend yield comes in below the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 1.2% yield. The dividend is quite secure, with the payout ratio likely to be in the mid-20% range for 2026. That should enable Moody's to keep handing out approximately 10% annual dividend growth (for a Chowder number of 11) to extend its 17-year dividend growth streak.

While Moody's is not extremely undervalued, it's still a solid value in our view. At the current $444 share price (as of April 7th, 2026), shares are priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 26.5. This is meaningfully less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 28.8, according to FAST Graphs.

Looking ahead, we think that a fair value P/E ratio of 28 is reasonable for Moody's. That's because its 12.3% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus demonstrates its fundamentals to be intact versus the 10-year CAGR of 12.5%.

Since we always prefer a margin of safety, we adjusted the fair value multiple one turn lower. This signals that even with a slight cushion, Moody's stock is trading at a 10% discount to our $482 fair value per share estimate.

If it lives up to growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could deliver a 20% total return through December 2027. By the end of 2031, Moody's would be primed for 12% annual total returns.

Of course, there are risks to the company's investment thesis.

One of the most notable risks to Moody's currently is the fact that it has aggressively moved into rating private credit and asset-backed finance. On the one hand, it's enabling strong business growth. On the other hand, it comes with reputational risk similar to the Great Recession if a widespread downturn hits the sector and its models don't properly account for the connection between private lenders and traditional banks.

A long-term risk is the possibility that AI becomes so efficient at credit analysis that the value premium charged for its ratings erodes. If proprietary models become commoditized, this could result in margin compression for Moody's. That could cause its valuation multiple to re-rate lower.

Conclusion

Moody's is not only an attractive company from a long-term growth viewpoint but also from their ability to continue to reward shareholders with their dividend growth. Moody's management is not blind to the fact that its share price has fallen in 2026. They've indicated, even in public conferences, that they have been heavily buying back shares from the beginning of this year and increased their share buyback plan by 25% from last year. This is a big boost to try and bring a bottom to their share price and reward current shareholders by removing those who want to exit. Shares that are bought back and retired help provide more value to every share that remains and provide more room for dividend growth because the overall cost of their dividend goes down with each share that they purchased.

Investing in companies that are well-known and hold dominant market positions can be highly rewarding, especially if you're willing to buy them only when they are fairly valued or undervalued. I don't believe in blindly buying a company just because it is well-known. I believe in buying exceptionally high-quality companies at fair or undervalued prices and enjoying the rewards of that position. That takes time, effort, and, most of all, clarity.

A Dividend King enjoys a position of clarity by rising above the noise.
